advantages: MSE is smooth, convex in ŷ, and yields closed-form solutions for linear regression; gradients are easy to compute.
MAE is robust to outliers and interpretable in the same units as y.
BCE and CE align training with probabilistic predictions and maximum likelihood under standard models.
CE provides strong gradients that penalize confident wrong classes — better than MSE on one-hot targets for classification.
All four losses decompose cleanly over examples, enabling mini-batch stochastic optimization at scale.
disadvantages: MSE over-penalizes outliers, which can distort models when a few labels are wrong or extreme.
MAE is non-differentiable at zero error (|·| corner), which can complicate some optimizers though subgradients work in practice.
BCE and CE blow up as predicted probability on the true class approaches zero (−log q → ∞).
None of these losses directly optimize ranking, fairness, or business metrics — you may need different objectives or post-hoc calibration.
Class imbalance can skew average CE/BCE unless weighted; raw losses favor frequent classes.
